title: SKIP LOCKED Claims, Lease Tokens, Fencing, and Multi-Instance Fairness · Contract Lens: Feed Ts Queue Fairness
summary: Replicas select the same due rows, transactions remain open during fetch, stale workers commit late data, heartbeats renew foreign work, and priority starves small tenants. Predict the reader decision, external-unknown boundary, Promise and AbortSignal ownership, PostgreSQL transaction, failure, and observable evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the feed ts queue fairness increment in fenced PostgreSQL fetch-claim protocol. Learners progress through pr…
objectives: Define due, priority, tenant, origin, failure, and starvation ordering with bounded batches and changed-load tests.
Finalize only when source, attempt, token, generation, state, and lease match and require exactly one affected row.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Define due, priority, tenant, origin, failure, and starvation ordering with bounded batches and changed-load tests.

hints: For Feed aggregation and TypeScript Defend the Feed Ts Queue Fairness model · Theory Feed Ts Queue Fair…, name the reader, publisher, source and emitted revision, Node, TypeScript, tenant, URL, unknown XML, runtime validation, Fetch, Promise, AbortSignal, PostgreSQL, Express, handle, accessibility, recovery, and native-transfer boundary, Feed Ts Queue Fairness precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Feed aggregation and TypeScript Defend the Feed Ts Queue Fairness model · Theory Feed Ts Queue Fair…, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A theory TypeScript team handles case 9928 during an inclusive cross-device research reader review. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove short SKIP LOCKED claims, fenced tokens, affected-row finalize, fairness, and lease faults; browser TypeScript must remain deterministic and free of Node, network, database, and host effects; then change one tenant, cursor, reader command, authorization, validator, sanitizer, or accessibility condition without leaking state. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, fast-xml-parser, Express, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, handle and load claims, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates.
